posts.title: Full Guide and Information for Theth Albania
posts.content:
For anyone who is interested in visiting the remote valley of Theth and cannot find enough information to plan your trip, you are in the right place! Below you with find a full guide on:
  1. Why should you visit Theth?
  2. How to go to Theth? Tirana - Theth transfer
  3. What to prepare for your trip.
  4. Things you must do in Theth.
  5. Best time of the year to visit Theth.
  6. Where to stay in Theth?
  7. Extend your adventure
1. Why should you visit Theth? Theth, the most majestic, isolated place in Albania, found deep in the Albanian Alps is a valley surrounded by mountains forming an ellipse shape region. Inside the valley you have pure wild nature, like waterfalls, canyons, rivers, the famous Blue Eye of Theth and forested hills—a perfect setting for hiking enthusiasts and nature lovers alike. Theth has the cleanest air, crystal-clear drinkable river water, mild summer temperatures, serene quietness and perfect picturesque views giving travellers an unparalleled retreat experience and peace of mind. At the heart of the valley lies Theth Village, a 16th-century settlement founded by locals to preserve their Catholic traditions during the Ottoman invasion. The village is home to an iconic Catholic church of Theth and the historically significant Reconciliation Tower, both rich in cultural and historical value. Domestic animals like sheep, goats, cows, pigs, donkeys and wild animals like foxes, stray horses, eagles are found roaming freely in the village, giving travellers the opportunity to come close and interact with them. If you want to relax and connect with nature, Theth is the place you must not miss in Albania. 2. How to go to Theth?  Tirana – Theth transfer As of this time (2025), there are three option you can choose to visit Theth.
  • 1. BUS
This is a very complicated method as there is no direct bus line from Tirana to Theth and cannot be done in 1 days. If you want to take a bus to Theth, you have to go to the North & South Albania Bus Terminal (search with this name on Google maps). You have to take a bus to the city Shkoder. The buses go every hours from 6 AM to 4-5 PM. Once you reach in Shkoder you have to stay overnight in Shkoder and catch the only bus that goes to Theth at 7 AM. This is a cheap method however, it is very time consuming, and it depends on the price on accommodation and food. However, you can contact a local tour operator, and they might offer a group transfer from Tirana to Theth or vice versa at a price of 40 EUR per person. Contact Albania Inbound (website) +355692380166. Most of the days you can find free seats during the day tour of Theth from Tirana. The transfer starts at 7 AM from Tirana and arrival in Theth is in 12 AM (with panoramic stops)
  • CAR RENTAL
Another method is to rent a car. This can make it possible for you to reach in one day and you will have the flexibility to stop wherever you want and travel around Theth conveniently. However there are two disadvantages when renting a car to go to Theth: -The road is scary for some people. The road is very narrow, and it climbs the mountain. Sometimes exchanging with a car can be tricky and nerve wrecking. -The cost of renting a car for 1 or several days can be more expensive then the other methods.
  • HIRE A LOCAL GUIDE OR BOOK A TOUR
Hiring a tour guide or going on a group tour is another method of visiting Theth. There are several tours that span from 1 to several days that cover Theth village. If you take a day tour, it will not be that expensive, you will have the luxury of an organised adventure and the support of a tour guide. However, going and coming from Theth in 1 day can take up to 13-14 hours. This may be tiring for some people but the option of dropping of in Theth is always an option. The company that does the most tours in Theth is Albania Inbound (Website) CONTACT +355692380166. You can reach out for more information.
  • BEST TIME AND MONEY EFFICENT TRANSFER TO THETH
If you want the most time and money efficient way of visiting Theth we suggest you take part in a day tour and pay for only a transfer (40 EUR per person). You can enjoy as many days you want in Theth and the day you decide to come back to Tirana, take part on the return journey of the day tour as a transfer to Tirana (40 EUR per person). The driver will drop you off on the requested location in Tirana. 3. What to prepare for your trip This question varies depending on weather conditions and the time of the year, however, in general the same things should be prepared. Generally: CASH (very important almost every service is purchased in cash), hiking shoes, hiking clothes, snacks for the road, warm clothes for evenings, offline maps. MAY - OCTOBER: Everything mentioned in “Generally”, sunscreen, swimming shorts. NOVEMBER – APRIL: Everything mentioned in “Generally”, warm clothes for the whole day, rain coat, chains for wheel in case of ice, more food in case of closed restaurants. 4. Things you must do in Theth. If you visit Theth, there are many things and activities you can take part however, I will list from the most important to the less but still important things that you must not miss in Theth.
  • 1. Find the Blue Eye
The most famous hike in Theth the Blue Eye hike. This is a 45-minute hike (one way) in the forest. During the hike you will come across many nature wonders like: Baths of Nderlysaj, Visdori Waterfall, Black River of Kaprre, wonderful rock formations during the hike, breathtaking picturesque views, and in the very end, the famous Blue Eye of Theth. After the tiring hike you will enjoy the freshest drinkable water of Albania. To reach the hiking start point you must drive with car from the village to Nderlysaj, and park in front of Restaurant Lulash Zelna. You will see the Nderlysaj Bridge from there. This is where the hiking starts. If you don’t have a car, you can either walk to the starting point which will take about 1 hour or take a local taxi for 5 EUR per person. A visit of Theth does not make sense if you don’t take part if this adventure.
  • 2. Find Grunas Waterfall
Grunas Waterfall is located near Grunas Canyons. A very beautiful 30 meters waterfall with stunning views of the valley. Also to reach in this wonder a hike is involved however shorter and easier then the Blue Eye hike. The hike starts near the Reconciliation Tower by the river. The path signs are not very clear but you will find many people going back and forth to the waterfall. Also, Offline Google maps from section 3 can help!
  • 3. Visit the Church of Theth and the Reconciliation Tower
The Church and the Tower are very important historical – cultural landmarks of Theth and also the whole North Albania region. The Church is special because of the picturesque view it offers and the Reconciliation Tower because it’s important role on the laws of North Albania Kanuni (Kanuni of Lek Dukagjini). You can often find a guide at the tower who can bring you in and explain in more detail.
  • 4. Ride the Thethi Zip Line
Theth Zipline is constructed by the road to Theth (15 mins drive) and if offers you a “short cut” to the village. The adventure lasts about 1.3 minutes, if offer a very impressive view of the village, comes close to the trees at one point for a bit of adrenaline and stops right in the centre of the village. This is a highly recommended adventure if you come to Theth.
  • 5. Small stop in Qafe Thore (during the road to Theth)
Qafe Thore is the peak of the mountain that you need to cross to reach Theth. The road of Qafe Thore is the Albania’s highest built road and if you stop by “Buni I Bajraktarit” you will experience one of the most beautiful views of the Albanian Alps and Albania as well. If you are driving to Theth, you must make a small stop in this site.
  • 6. Bath in the river of Theth
What can be a more refreshing experience that bathing in the what is regarded as the coldest water in Albania in a hot sunny day. The Theth river flows right by the village. Why go to the sea when you have such a beautiful and refreshing spot! 5. Best time of the year to visit Theth. As a local tour operator with Albanian expert travel agents in our team, the believe that the best time of the year to travel to Theth for the ultimate experience would be the months of May, June and September. The temperatures are warm but not hot, there is the perfect number of travellers around, not to make it overcrowded or a complete desert, prices are reasonable, and you can enjoy all activities this time period. During other months, Theth is still a wonderful place to visit but sometimes overcrowded, sometimes very hot and sometimes very expensive! 6. Where to stay in Theth? You can book accommodation in Theth via booking.com. You can find shared rooms for cheaper price or book a private room with bathroom. Prices per bed are around 20-25 EUR per bed and for private room with bathroom from 65-85 EUR. 7. Extend your adventure If you are a real lust for adventure and what to explore pure wild nature, there is one more thing you can do in Theth. As you might know, The Albanian Alps are full of small villages like Theth that are separated by mountain ranges. There is a trail, probably the most famous in Albania, that connects Theth village to Valbone. This is a 8 hour hike that goes beyond the Blue Eye and gives you a first hand experience on the untouched beauty of Albania. Once you finish this hike you will reach in Valbone, the adventure does not stop here…. The best way to go back to civilization (Shkoder) is to go by boat via Shala River. What can be a better ending that going back on a boat adventure and visiting more wild areas. However, be cautious, the Valbona – Theth trail is NOT RECOMMENDED to be completed alone if you are not an experienced hiker. It is better to hire a local tour guide to help you cross

posts.title: Top 10 Interesting Facts about Albania
posts.content: INTRODUCTION TO ALBANIA Probably you have never heard of Albania, or even if you have, I bet nothing good has been communicated to you. Pretty understandable. We are talking about an ethnicity that out of 10 million people, 7.5 million live outside of Albania. Our history has not been easy. From the Roman empire times to the Ottoman empire our culture was supressed. Even after the Ottomans we experienced the hardest form of communism in Europe. We were the “North Korea” of Europe With the much-expected implementation of Democracy, finally these days are long gone. Now Albania is looking forward to joining the rest of the countries of Europe and become part of the European Union. Spanning 28,748KM2 with a population of 2.4 million, Albania is located in the Balkan peninsula bordering Greece South, North Macedonia and Kosovo East, Montenegro North and the Adriatic and Ionian Sea West. Top 10 Interesting Facts about Albania Population: Where are the Albanians? The population of Albania is 2.5 million people. However, the total Albanian population is 10 million. This means that 75% of the population lives outside of its home country! This is caused especially by the mass immigration after the communist regime fell. Bunkers: Albania has 273 thousand bunkers!! During the height of the communist times, the notorious dictator Enver Hoxha fortified the country for a probabilistic upcoming, which never came. Now there are 273.000 bunkers, 1 per 9 people!!! Flag: A two headed eagle with a red background. Albania has one of the most unique flags in the world. The flag depicts a black two headed eagle in a red background. The red background stands for bloodshed, bravery, and honour while the eagle is a symbol of Albania’s strength and sovereignty. The eagle is a signature symbol and animal in Albania. Coffee shops: The country with the densest coffee shops in the world! The easiest this to do in Albania is to find a coffeehouse. You can walk by ANY road in Albania and will find one, or even several. There are about 18000 coffeeshops in Albania, 1 per 130 people, making Albania the country with the densest coffeeshops per capita in the world!! Religion: Officially the first ATHEIST country in history! Following the communist ideology and theory, the dictator of Albania, Enver Hoxha, banned all forms of religion in 1969, officially declaring Albania the first atheist country in history. Fortunately, the ban was lifted after communism fell. Now Albania is a place where people form different religions live in peace and harmony. Language: One of the oldest languages in the world with no origin! Albanian is a separate branch of the Indo-European tree of languages. Linguistic experts find it very hard to compare the Albanian language with any of the present languages and have determined from the language structure that Albanian must be very old and one of the first languages to be used in Europe. Albanian Riviera: A hidden paradise right in Europe’s backyard! Tourism in Albania has been developing only after the COVID-19 pandemic hit. Travelers were eager to explore new places and they came along to Albania, only to find one of the beach shorelines in the world! The Albanian Riviera spans from Vlore to Ksamil, hosting beautiful beach sites with crystal clear waters. You can drive by the awe-inspiring landscapes where hills meet the shoreline. There are several beach villages like: Himare, Jale, Borsh, Lukove etc. The most famous of them being: Dhermi, Ksamil Islands and Saranda City. Albanian Currency: LEK Albania has it’s own currency called the Albanian Lek. (LEK) comes from the medieval leader Lek Dukagjini, the most important figure of culture and history for the north Albanian mountains villages – tribes. LEK is the basic form of currency with QINDARKA being its /100th devision (1 LEK = 100 QINDARKA). However, qindarka now are only part of history as they are so small in value they are not used anymore. EUR is widely accepted in Albania as a form of currency. If you have EUR with you, you can purchase almost anything. At this point, 1 EUR is equal to 98 LEK. National drink: RAKIA, only for the strong. Rakia is the traditional alcoholic beverage of Albania. It is a very strong hard liquor drink that is used mostly among the older population. It is a tradition that Albanians drink coffee and rakia in the morning to start the day. Rakia has about 50% alcohol concentration and it’s made locally by villagers. It can be made from different fruits like: grapes, cherries, plumps etc. You can taste how strong it is just by smelling it… drink with caution! Transportation: Mercedez-Benz Take a walk in the roads of Tirana (the Capital) and you will immediately understand this section. There is an overwhelming number of Mercedez-Benz cars in Albania in comparison with other bands. Albanians love their cars and they choose a Benz model in the majority of the cases, making this brand more of a status symbol in Albania. The young

posts.title: Best activities to do in Albania
posts.content:
1.Head South. Visit and retreat by the Albanian Riviera One of the most beautiful regions in Albania are the serene beaches you find in the costal are from Vlore to Ksamil. Here are some of the top beaches in the area:
  • Dhermi
  • Jale These beaches are perfect for young visitors seeking vibrant nightlife, loud music, and lively party scenes.
  • Himare
  • Borsh
  • Lukova These spots are ideal for families with young children or older couples looking for a peaceful retreat in a quiet seaside village.
  • Saranda
  • Ksamil These two destinations are the most popular in the region and are must-visits for their vibrant atmosphere and unique charm
2.Visit Cultural Places Albania is a country who has a lot of history and culture spanning from Classical Antiquity (10 BCE to Medieval Period and Modern History (20th century). There are several important historic sites you must visit in Albania, some of which being UNESCO world heritage sites like: -Berat -Gjirokastra Berat and Gjirokastra are UNESCO protected cities, both mostly constructed during the Ottoman period (Medieval). Both of the cities have a castle dating back to Roman period and Old towns and city centres with the typical Ottoman architecture. This two cities are a must visit if you are visiting Albania. -Apollonia -Butrinti Apollonia and Butrinti are again 2 UNESCO sites, different from the two previous cities, this two flourished during the Roman and Greek period (Classical Antiquity). In these cities you will find ancient theatres, Roman baths, acropolis, and many typical constructions of the time. Fun Fact: The first roman emperor (Augustus) studied in Apollonia under the tutelage of Athenodorus of Tarsus. For those who are interested in how the ancient world developed in these regions, you must visit Apollonia and Butrint. -Kruja City Kruja is the most important historic city in Albania as it is the center of Albanian resistance against the Ottoman invaders. Also being the stronghold of Skanderbeg, this would be a great place to learn about the Albanian history. 3. Explore Tirana City Tirana is a modern metropolis who has experienced a vast improvement the past years. There are a lot of things you can do to make yourself busy in Tirana, some of them being: -Visiting BUNKART 1 BUNKART 1 is the largest bunker in country, now turned into a museum you can visit to learn about the harsh communism history of Albania. -Dajti Express Dajti Express is the longest cable car in the Balkans. It starts very near the BUNKART 1 museum, making it a perfect combination with BUNKART 1 to visit in one day. -Bovilla Lake Bovilla Lake is a man-made reservoir 20 KM from Tirana, powering the metropolis electricity and water supply. The turquoise water with the sharp green hills makes this destination a favourite destination among travellers. -Enjoy a traditional lunch in ODA restaurant If you want to experience very delicious traditional food in Albania, you must go to ODA restaurant. This is one of the most famous restaurants in Tirana because its traditional food, music and atmosphere. -The Grand Park of Tirana If you are in Tirana, you must go for a walk on the grand park of the city. The lake of Tirana surrounded with the forest of the park makes walking by it an amazing peaceful experience -Visit TEG (Tirana East Gate) TEG, the biggest shopping mall in the Balkans is situated east of Tirana by Tirana - Elbasan highway. In TEG you can take part in different activities such as: shopping, watching a movie in the cinema, going on restaurants and much more. -Go on a walking tour The best way to explore Tirana’s centre attractions is through a walking tour. You will cover several landmarks like: Skanderbeg Square, The Pyramid of Tirana, Tirana boulevard etc -Go for a drink in Tirana Castle When you hear the words castle and drinking together you may get a little bit confused, but this is not your typical castle. Situated near the centre of the city, the remaining of the castle of Tirana boasts a number of coffee shops and lounges that create a vibrating atmosphere on the afternoon. 4. Go for a hike on the Albanian Alps One of the best activities you can do in Albania is enjoying the wild mountain nature. There are many beautiful places in Albania where you can do hiking activities to explore the nature in a deeper level. Some of the hikes you can do are: -Hike to the Blue Eye of Theth -Theth – Valbona cross -Mount Korab Summit -Gjipe Canyon hike -Osumi Canyon hike -Many other trails on the Albanian Alps 5. Experience the Village Life Albania has a lot of agricultural land spanning across the country. One of the best places to experience the village life is Theth Village. More information about Theth: (LINK OF OTHER BLOG) 6. Experience the wild nature of Shala River with a boat tour Save one day to go to Shala River and Komani Lake. The area around is referred to as the Thailand of Europe. Breathtaking natural beauty. 7. Take part in water activities There are a lot of water activities in Albania. Some of them are: -Rafting or Kayaking in Vjosa River These activities are organised in Permet village. A nice combination of fun activities and the quiet atmosphere of Permet. -Boat Tour to Karaburun Peninsula If you are in Vlore, you cannot miss Karaburun Peninsula. Accessible only by boat, there are full day boat tours offered in Vlora port for people who want to join. -Jet ski / Pedal boats in Ksamil One of the most beautiful aspects of Ksamil if that is has 3 islands not more then 100 meters away from its shores. Imagine taking your time exploring the islands on a pedal boat. 8. Try the traditional foods and drinks Albania offers a variety of traditional food, and a special drink called “Rakia”. If you are confused on what foods to try, here is a list: -Byrek Byrek is the number one pick for breakfast. The best Byrek you can find is on the smallest shops! -Ferges -Bakllava -Try goat meat If you wish to try this traditional dish, book a table to ODA restaurant. -Notoriously fresh vegetables and fruits Fresh hand-picked vegetables and food nowadays are getting rare. All travellers that visit Albania are amazed by the taste of the vegetables. Enjoy while you can!

posts.title: Full Albanian Experience with Albania Inbound Tour Operator
posts.content:
Full Albanian Experience with Albania Inbound Tour Operator It's time for you to arrange your travel in Albania, the beautiful exotic country on the tip of the Balkan peninsula. Information can be limited sometimes and arranging a travel in an unfamiliar country and culture can be a hustle. There are many things you must do to really feel and experience the culture of Albania. For those who are interested in a full experience, you are in the right place. We have designed a 10-day program for the purpose of introducing travelers to every aspect of Albania, from wild natural wonders on the Albanian Alps, cultural and historical landmarks, authentic experiences to the pristine crystal-clear waters of Albanian Riviera. During the day, activities are included in the program to help you explore every corner of the country in a unique way that will make the experience fun and enjoyable. And during the night, comfortable accommodation in the city center to make it easy for travelers to visit during the evening. Full Albanian Experience: A 10 Day Tour program.   (PDF file download brochure) See below the best parts of the itinerary.   Top Places & Activities of the program:
  1. Hiking in the Albanian Alps
  2. Boat tour in Shala River
  3. E-bike tour of Tirana
  4. Cultural visits
  5. Traditional food
  6. Outdoor adventures
  7. Local wine Tasting in Berat
  8. Water activities in Permet
  9. Beach time by the Albanian Riviera
  10. Interaction with local people
  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  1. Hiking in the Albanian Alps
Travelers will have the opportunity to go on a nature exploration in Theth and Shala River, Permet etc. During the stay in Theth, you will embark on a 2 hours guided hike on the wild nature of the mountains to reach the secluded Blue Eye of Theth. One of the most beautiful nature wonders of Albania. Another short, guided hike (30 minutes) will take place to find the Grunas waterfall. Full outdoor adventure.    
  1. Boat tour in Shala River
Shala River and Komani Lake are regarded as the Thailand of Albania. A Thailand in the middle of the mountains. Travelers will have the opportunity to get into a boat with a local and sail 1.5 hours by Shala River in between mountains to reach a wild place called Zgiboze. An adventure they will never forget.    
  1. E-bike tour of Tirana
Probably you are bored of the typical walking tour of the city and sometimes it is a bit physically demanding. Why not take an electric bike to explore and have fun!    
  1. Cultural visits
One of the goals of the program is to educate travelers about the history and culture of Albania. This is why many cultural visits are predicted with a full tour of the site. Some of the best sites are: Berat, Gjirokastra, Butrint, Kruja, Shkoder... basically all historic sites are covered!    
  1. Traditional food
Each day, lunch is expected to be at small local restaurants where you can sample Albanian traditional food and beverages. Rakia, a traditional alcoholic drink, is often served with the meal, proceed with caution....    
  1. Outdoor adventures
This program is not created to be boring. Travelers will be part of many outdoor adventures like: guided hiking tours in the Albanian Alps, nature exploring in Zgiboze, visiting a hot spring and Vjosa river in Permet, finding the Blue Eye of Saranda, exploring the Albanians Riviera most beautiful and untouched shores, and much more.    
  1. Local wine Tasting in Berat
Near Berat there are a lot of small villages that own hills and hills of vineyards. Travelers will have the opportunity to visit one of them, Peshtan and try the local wine in Pupa winery.    
  1. Water activities in Permet
Permet is a small town located by Vjosa river, where travelers will be transported to enjoy some time. The river has formed canyons and where several water activities take place like: Rafting, Kayaking etc. Near the river after crossing an Ottoman bridge you can find a beautiful thermal bath spot.    
  1. Beach time by the Albanian Riviera
Albania is most famous for its beautiful South coast. During this program, travelers will have the opportunity to visit several beach villages like: Lukove, Himare, Dhermi and enjoy a relaxing and fun time by one of the clearest waters in Europe.    
  1. Interaction with local people
The local taverns where you can stop for lunch are small, family-owned businesses run by polite and welcoming hosts. Travelers will have the opportunity to engage in conversations with them, as Albanians are known for their friendliness and love of good conversation!

posts.title: Shala River in Albania – Paradise on Earth
posts.content: One of the best places to choose to visit in Albania and one of the most beautiful sites not just in Albania but in Europe is Komani Lake, the region where Shala River flows. Situated approximately: 130 KM from Tirana, 141 KM from Durres, 54 KM from Shkoder, the river is found deep into the Albanian Alps. To reach the Komani port, you need to enter a 400-meter tunnel. After crossing the tunnel, you will hit a wall of people. The port is very dense with people and boats. There are several companies that offer different kind of boat trips in Komani, with the most famous one and the standard being the boat trip to Zgiboze. Once you leave the port by 9:30 AM, the quietness and beauty of nature will sink in. You will experience a breath taking 90 minutes boat ride by Shala River in between mountains to one of the most remote, wild nature sites in Albania. You will reach in Zgiboze at about 11 AM and you will have a full 4 hours to experience and explore the region.     Zgiboze is a beach like region but in the mountains and a very high altitude. The terrain to some people looks like the beach areas in Thailand, this is why many people call it “The Thailand of Europe”. The beauty of the Shala River is beyond words—come and experience it for yourself! At 3 PM the boats all together return to the port. Try to make memories while you can!
posts.meta_title: Shala River in Albania – Paradise on Earth

posts.title: All you need to know before you travel to Albania
posts.content: Albania is a beautiful and unique country. It has mountains, beaches, kind people, good food, and a mix of old and new. If you’re planning a trip here, there are a few things you should know to make your journey easier and more fun. 1. Bring Cash With You Even though Albania is growing fast and many places in Tirana accept cards, this is not the case everywhere. Small cafés, shops, and markets mostly take cash, especially outside the capital. If you plan to visit villages or smaller towns, always carry enough cash with you.   2. Albanians Are Very Helpful If you ever get lost or need help, don’t be afraid to ask. Albanians are known for being friendly and caring. Many times, people will come to help even before you ask. If not, just ask someone nearby, they will often bring others to help too!   3. Driving Is… Passionate Planning to rent a car? Make sure you are confident behind the wheel. Drivers in Albania don’t always follow the rules, and traffic can seem wild. But don’t worry too much, locals somehow understand each other and rarely get angry while driving.   4. Coffee Shops Are Everywhere If you love coffee, you’ll love Albania. There are cafés on almost every corner. Albanians enjoy meeting friends at coffee shops, and the coffee is really good. You’ll always find a place to relax and enjoy a drink.   5. Weather Is Soft and Simple Albania has mild weather. Winters are not too cold and summers are not too hot. A warm jacket is enough for winter, and in the summer, light clothes and a bottle of water will keep you comfortable. No need to overpack!   6. Know Where You Want to Go Albania offers different things depending on where you go: • For mountains and nature, visit the north (like the Albanian Alps). • For history and culture, visit cities like Shkoder, Kruje, Berat, and Gjirokastra. • For beautiful beaches, go southwest to the Albanian Riviera. • For city life, stay in Tirana, which is in the center of the country.   7. Book Early in Summer Tourism is growing fast in Albania, but there are still not enough hotels and restaurants—especially in summer. If you’re traveling between June and September, book your rooms and dinner spots as early as you can.   8. Don’t Drink Tap Water In some countries, tap water is safe to drink. In Albania, it’s not. Always buy bottled water to drink and use tap water only for washing or brushing your teeth.   9. English Is Spoken Often You don’t need to worry too much about language. Most young Albanians speak good English. Even older people may not speak it perfectly, but they will still try to help. You can usually get your message across without much trouble.   10. Bus Travel Is Difficult If you want to travel by bus, plan very carefully. Buses in Albania are few, often late, and not always in the best condition. There are not many signs or clear timetables. It’s best to ask locals or book ahead if you can. Albania is a hidden gem in Europe. It’s full of charm, adventure, and warm-hearted people. With a little planning and the right expectations, you will have an unforgettable experience.
posts.meta_title: All you need to know before you travel to Albania

posts.title: How to Obtain Four Passport Stamps When Traveling to Albania
posts.content:
When you are traveling to a new country you want to do special activities. When a country is small and bordered by other small nations, like Albania, taking day trips to its neighboring countries can be an exciting option. This is a good idea if you want a quick check in to a new country or an extra stamp in your passport.

In Albania you can get 3 other new stamps, from: North Macedonia, Kosovo, Montenegro.

  1. Day Trip to Ohrid, North Macedonia: One of the most beautiful UNESCO sites and a short 2.5 hours drive from Tirana is Ohrid. Situated by Ohrid Lake, Ohrid is a city where time seems to stand still. Ohrid is renowned for its 365 churches, each reflecting the influence of various cultures from the Roman Empire to the Ottoman Empire, and more. A tip for this day would be to drive alongside the lake and explore Saint Naum Monastery. You can cross the border to re-enter Albania on the other side of the lake and visit Pogradec City also. Have a nice day visiting Lake Ohrid on both sides.   2. Day Trip to Prizren & Prishtina, Kosovo Another option is to take a tour to the brother country of Albania, Kosovo. The border is being removed between Albania and Kosovo so probably you will not get a stamp, but still a good selfie with a check-in is an option. Prishtina is the capital of Kosovo with Prizren being its cultural capital. Prizren is a short 2.5 hours’ drive from Tirana. You can explore the old town of Prizren and enjoy a lunch in the traditional taverns of the city. Another 1-hour drive will bring you to Prishtina. In Prishtina you can see the city centre and enjoy a walk around.   3. Day Trip to Budva & Kotor, Montenegro Budva and Kotor are situated by the Adriatic Sea and use to be pirate cities. The beautiful Venetian architecture of the old towns and their location by the pristine waters of the Adriatic Sea makes them one of the most stunning attractions of the region. Along the drive there are several places worth for a short stop like Sveti Stefan. Budva is reached after 3.5 hours’ drive and Kotor only 30 minutes from Budva. These destinations are a bit further then the previous two from Tirana, however it will be forth it.
posts.meta_title: How to Obtain Four Passport Stamps When Traveling to Albania

posts.title: Top sunset spots in Tirana you will want to keep a secret
posts.content: We all want to go somewhere peaceful to enjoy our afternoon coffee under the sunset, to read our favorite book, to spend a nice time with friends or loved ones, or simply to be alone and take a break from everyday routine. Well, now you don’t have to think too much about where to go.  Here is a list of the best cafés in Tirana, from quiet book cafés to lively community spots, where everyone can find their perfect place. 1. Le Chateau A French chateau in the heart of Tirana, where the beauty of the past and the present come together in a French style. A cozy spot with amazing drinks, friendly staff and fun games. The perfect place for a relaxing break and for studying too. Once you go, you’ll want to come back again and again. 2. Antigua Speciality Coffee If you think coffee is just a drink to wake you up, maybe you haven’t tried 100% Arabica artisan roasted from Antigua yet. A quiet and comfortable place with a nice interior and small cozy tables inside and outside. The perfect spot to read a book while enjoying your coffee. 3. 505 Café For all Arctic Monkeys fans, 505 Café is the perfect place for anyone who wants to go back to the past, to a traditional and cozy home where small things make you feel complete. It has a lively atmosphere mixed with a unique design that gives a bit of nostalgia, just like the song’s message. Warm space, friendly service, and a carefully made menu – what more could you want? 4. Destil Creative Hub The perfect place to enjoy a Sunday morning breakfast while listening to good music or working in the coworking area. A unique and lively atmosphere, good food, delicious desserts, friendly and caring staff. Everyone here is ready to make your experience unforgettable. 5. N’Dritëro As the name says, it’s a reading corner, inspired by Dritëro Agolli, a big name in Albanian literature. When you enter, you feel like you’re in a world full of books and stories. An intimate place where poetry, wine, and books come together closely. Experience the spirit of Tirana’s literature at Dritëro. 6. Hana Corner Café With a location very close to the city center, at Hana Corner Café, you can find the peace you need in the noisy streets of Tirana. It’s a cozy place with great coffee and cakes. The staff is very friendly and helpful. Hana is more than e café, it’s a community. 7. Frut’za One of the healthiest places you should try in Tirana for your healthy breakfast. Even if you’re not a regular juice drinker, Frutza’s menu might change your mind. They offer a variety of fruit juices, detox shots, and smoothies. Let’s not even start talking about the special coffee that leaves a taste of chocolate after you drink it, or the olive oil coffee with olive oil macchiatos and cappuccinos, something many of you probably haven’t tried yet. At Frut’za, you can start your day full of energy.
posts.meta_title: Top sunset spots in Tirana you will want to keep a secret

posts.title: A journey along the Albanian Riviera: The cleanest and hidden beaches
posts.content: Summer is coming, and with it comes the big question: where will you spend your holidays? When we hear “summer vacation,” one word always comes to mind…the sea. But holidays are more than just time off. They’re a chance to escape the routine, leave stress behind, and reconnect with yourself. Even a few days away can refresh your mind and bring back your lost energy. If you haven’t picked your destination yet, don’t worry, we’ve a solution for you. Below, you’ll find some of the hidden gems of Southern Albania. Crystal waters, peaceful views, and unforgettable moments are waiting for you.   Krorëza Beach is one of the cleanest and most beautiful beaches in Albania. The view is stunning. It’s a place where nature feels untouched, the human hand hasn’t changed it yet. If you’re looking for a quiet, intimate spot with no bars or buildings, just bring your camping gear and enjoy the magic this place has to offer.     Aquarium Beach (Himarë) About 1.5 km north of Himara, hidden behind pine trees and rocks, Aquarium Beach is one of the perfect spots for an unforgettable experience. With crystal water, white sand, and stunning views, it feels like a magical place. For adventure lovers, you can even jump into the water from the surrounding rocks. And if you prefer quiet beaches without crowds, this is the place for you.     Gjipe Beach Between Dhermi and Jale, about 3 km off the Llogara road, lies Gjipe Beach — hidden in a stunning canyon known as the Gjipe Canyon. It’s one of the true gems of the Albanian Riviera. The water blends beautiful shades of blue and green and is incredibly clear. If you're looking for total peace, camping under the stars, and unforgettable views without the crowds… this is the perfect place for you.     Grama Bay (Karaburun) Located on the western coast of the Karaburun Peninsula, Grama Bay is one of the most historic bays in Albania. Surrounded by high cliffs, you can still see ancient Illyrian writings carved into the rocks, a true treasure for history lovers. Whether you’re looking for a quiet day of camping or a unique experience far from everything, Grama Bay is one of the most beautiful places in all of Albania.     Kakome Beach  Surrounded by green hills on the Ionian coast, between the village of Nivice and Krorëza Beach, you’ll find Kakome Beach. With crystal-clear water in shades of blue and green, untouched nature, and a peaceful, private atmosphere, it’s the perfect place to relax. Ideal for spending quiet days by the sea with the people you love…far from the noise, close to nature.

posts.title: Explore Bovilla Reservoir - The secret blue paradise near Tirana
posts.content:
Do you need to get away from the bustle of the city for a moment and feel the essence of a unique natural experience? Don’t think twice about exploring Bovilla Reservoir! Just 20km from Tirana, Bovilla Reservoir is a breathtaking lake where you can discover great viewpoints. For adventurers, pleasant hiking, breathtaking photos, and creating travel memories.  A touch of history  Bovilla is an artificial lake, constructed in the 1980s to supply drinking water to the capital. By the time it became an immense lake beside Tirana, it made a valuable contribution to its capital infrastructure, but remarkably today it has also become a place of great satisfaction for hiking, walking, or immersing ourselves in the untouched landscape of Albanian nature.  How to get there? (Step by step) 
  1. Departure from Tirana –Begin your journey from the center of Tirana and set off heading north in the direction of the Casa Italia roundabout (a well-known landmark).
  2. Head toward Kamëz and the Agricultural University of Tirana. - From Casa Italia, follow the main road going straight in the direction of Kamëz. After passing through Kamëz, you will arrive at the Agricultural University of Tirana (Universiteti Bujqësor i Tiranës) located on the right-hand side on the road. This is a significant point to note on your trip.
  3. Drive toward Zall Herr - After the Agricultural University, continue straight on the main road until reaching the village of Zall Herr. This is a small village with a few local shops along the way.
  4. Turn onto Hida Rd (in Zall Herr) Once you have passed through the center of Zall Herr, you will see a small intersection (with signage of “Hida Rd”). Follow the road, which starts out paved, but soon stops and becomes a dirt road.
  1. Dirt Section (about 5-6 km)- After the paved roadway ends, you will be driving on a 5-6 km long dirt road that is very rough with potholes, rocks, and tight curves. This section of the drive will require slow moving, patience, and is best done in a 4x4 vehicle or SUV.
  2. Arrival at the Bovilla Dam - At the end of the dirt road, you will encounter a small bridge crossing part of the lake that will provide a scenic view of the turquoise waters. Just past the bridge, you will arrive at the dam. There will be a small parking area at this point.
  3. Continuing Uphill (about 1 km) -You can continue up from the dam on a more rough and steep dirt road for about 1 km. This section is off-road and will need much care to drive. It is steep, on loose rocks, has some sharp bends, and you will have to be qualified in this type of driving. Otherwise it may be easier to walk or take a group tour.
  1. Arrive at Bar-Restaurant Bovilla - At the top of Mount Gamti, you’ll find the Bar-Restaurant Bovilla, which offers food, drinks, and a place to relax. From here, you’ll enjoy an amazing view of the turquoise Bovilla Lake and the bright green hills that drop steeply into the water.
  2. Short hike to the panoramic viewpoint - From the restaurant, you can take a 10-minute walk (some stairs included) that leads to a viewpoint at the very top of Mount Gamti. From there, you’ll have a spectacular 360-degree view of the entire lake and the surrounding mountains. You got it!
Practical Tips
  • Check your vehicle before you leave, especially your spare tire.
  • Always take water and some snacks.
  • Wear appropriate shoes for hiking and consider wearing a hat for sun protection.
  • Although you may not feel comfortable driving to the location on your own, Albania Inbound provides safe, organized transport with explanations from an expert guide that is knowledgeable about the area.
What to expect?  On arrival, you will be welcomed with peaceful, clear waters surrounded by rolling, green mountains. Whether you are wandering the dam or climbing the hills, there will be an incredible place to take in the views and take photos to impress your friends. Turn it into an adventure with Albania Inbound  Bovilla Reservoir is included in our sequence of tour itineraries: 
  • Hiking & Trekking for all levels - we can get you in those epic views!
  • Day Tours with lakeside relaxation.
  • Photo Tours to capture magical moments.
  • Combo Tours with Bovilla Reservoir and exceptional stop at Kruja city, all on the same amazing day! 
Bovilla is much more than a lake, it is your likely-to-be-favourite nature getaway, hidden gem just waiting for you. Come to see it with Albania Inbound and make memories that will last a lifetime.
posts.meta_title: Explore Bovilla Reservoir - The secret blue paradise near Tirana

posts.title: The Blue Eye in Saranda – A natural wonder that invites you to look beyond the surface
posts.content:
Albania has a lot of untouched and breathtaking natural beauty, and one of its most beautiful gems is without a doubt The Blue Eye in Saranda (Syri i Kaltër in Albanian). This natural spring, with its almost surreal colors and feeling, is not just a site, it's an experience. A site where time feels like it ceases to exist and nature communicates in silence. A little background  The Blue Eye in Saranda is a karst spring with high pressure that bubbles up from deep underground. We don’t know how deep the spring is; even divers who reached more than 50 meters down did not hit the bottom. From above, the water resembles a giant eye; there is a navy blue center with turquoise and emerald rings wrapping around it. You can see why it was named the Blue Eye. Culturally, the mythology says this was the eye of a mythical creature that once watched over nature. This place still feels mythological and spiritual today. How to get there? The Blue Eye is only about 20 km from the coastal city of Saranda traveling on the road to Gjirokastër, and can be reached: - By car: It is drivable, although the last part of the road is bit away from standard. - By a tour company: We here at Albania Inbound encourage taking one of our day trips (Gjirokastra & Blue Eye Day Tour) or multi-day tour, which includes pre-packaged tours to the Blue Eye in Saranda and many other magnificent places in southern Albania. Our guides offer a wealth of local knowledge, and you will have an enjoyable trip, with added stops and more comfort. - By bike or motorbike: For the more adventurous, this option is best for the scenic countryside. The travel itself can be just as magical as the destination, with everywhere you turn olive groves, hills, and unspoiled nature. Why you should visit? -It is completely unique - The Blue Eye in Saranda is a very rare natural phenomenon. The bright blue  colour and crystal-clear water as well as the view of nature, no spring in Albania or the Balkans is anything close to a sight like this! A refreshing getaway into nature - Once you're payed for your ticket to the site, you'll see the green and tranquil scenery. It is a good escape from the noise and stress of the city. Additionally, once you dip a toe into the cool and pure water, along with the fresh air will completely awaken your senses. - A photographer's dream - Whether you are a professional or you just love taking photos, you will see something that will spark every type of photo imaginable! The shades of bright blue and green colour, along with the sun shining through the trees will create a very beautiful photos.  - Culturally and Legendary charged - The site is not only a natural phenomenon but is full of legends. The fantasies and folklore add a mystical aspect to the experience and significance that allows you to connect with the land more deeply. Great for all types of travel  - Whether you are a solo traveler, traveling with family, or a group, this site is welcoming and easy to enjoy. While the walking distance from the entrance to the spring is not very long, it is unknown when you'll be able to sit and enjoy the view.  Close to other sites - It's location Travel Tips:
  • Arrive early to beat the crowds and catch the morning light off the water.
  • Take water, snacks, and a hat for the sun, especially in the summer.
  • Swimming is not allowed in the main spring for safety and environmental purposes.
  • Wear comfortable shoes for the short walk from the parking area.
Explore with Albania Inbound At Albania Inbound, if you're going to the Blue Eye and want to leave the planning to someone else, we provide tours and experiences that cater to all your traveling needs. Adventure in nature or culture and food experiences nearby,  we make sure you get the most out of your journey. Start your travel experience with Albania Inbound and see the Blue Eye and the local treasures around it.  And remember, Albania is calling, you will just see the Blue Eye first!

posts.title: Albania's castles - A journey of stone and time
posts.content: If stones could narrate their own stories, the castles of Albania could recite some remarkable tales of bravery and courageous victories, of heart-wrenching romance, and of enduring folklore. These castles can be found all over the country, from the green hills of Berat to the blue beaches of Himara and they are much more than just old buildings. They inspire new visitors to engage with the essence of the land.  And at the centre of this remarkable mosaic of stone is surely the castle of Krujë, a symbol of success and fight against oppression, and one of the most interesting places to visit for anyone trying to connect with history. 1.Kruja Castle – The beating heart of albanian pride  Soaring on a steep hillside with breathtaking views down the hills towards the plains of Tirana and northwards to the Adriatic Sea's horizon, Kruja Castle is more than simply a medieval castle; it's a site of immense meaning. This was the stronghold of Gjergj Kastrioti Skanderbeg, Albania's national hero who valiantly resisted the overpowering forces of the Ottoman Empire. Inside the thick, weather-beaten castle walls rests the Skanderbeg Historical Museum featuring the most modern installations and immersive displays that depict life as it must have been during the 15th-century struggle. Close to the museum is the Ethnographic Museum set in an 18th century Ottoman period house that shows more about the rhythm of traditional Albanian living. The stone-paved paths to and from the castle are populated by local artists, handmade carpets, and wonderful aromas of mountain teas, which combine together to relay a theme of heritage and taste embedded in living history. 2.Berat Castle – A Live Piece of History  Referred to as the “City of a Thousand Windows,” Berat Castle is one of the few enclaves in the world still home to families creating living heritage. Its origins date to the 4th century BCE, with architectural modifications and expansions during the Byzantine and Ottoman periods. Once inside the fortress walls, visitors will discover today’s Orthodox churches as well as virtually intact frescoes and the Onufri Museum devoted to the most famous icon painter in Albania. Climb to the top of the castle's ramparts for spectacular views of the Osum River and all the white-washed houses of Berat in the distance and hovering over the horizon Mount Tomorr. Berat Castle allows for a tranquil environment to proceed through history, spirituality, and beautiful landscapes 3.Gjirokastër Castle - The southern stone giant  Gjirokastër castle is the dominant feature of a UNESCO-listed town of Gjirokastër, and expands more than 330 meters. Gjirokastër castle is one of the largest and most dominating castles in Albania. It was first built in the 4th century, and was later enlarged in the 19th Century during the rule of the infamous Ottoman Ali Pasha of Tepelena.  Within the castle's walls are:  -The National Museum of Armaments which displays weapons documenting Albania's extensive resistance history.   - A downed American fighter jet, rapport de la guerre froide!   - A communist prison, which held political prisoners until the 1980s. Creepy, but important for understanding modern Albanian history.   - The fortress also hosts The National Folk Festival - the festival of Albanian traditional music and dance - every few years, taking over the fortress and transforming this historic place into a stage of cultural pride. 4.Rozafa Castle - The meeting of myth and history  Perched atop a rocky hill overlooking the convergence of the Buna, Drin, and Kir rivers just outside of Shkodër, Rozafa Castle has observed the rise and fall of the Romans, Byzantines, Venetians, and Ottomans and has been around since the Illyrian era! However, what makes this castle extraordinary is its famed legend: that of Rozafa, a woman who voluntarily allowed herself to be buried to ensure the fortress would stand firm. Today, locals say her outline can still be seen in the stone - a remarkable reminder of love, sacrifice, and fortitude. Dedications are in order for visitors: The ruins of St. Stephen's Church. Evidence of all the peoples layered into its walls. A small museum space on the inside. If you're mindful of the height, the views from the walls are sweeping with glimpses of Lake Shkodra, its surroundings, and the Albanian alps on clear days. A sunset here is a poem. 5. Porto Palermo Castle – The seaside fortress Unlike the other castles on the list, Porto Palermo Castle is a unique coastal treasure. Situated on the stunning Albanian Riviera (near Himara), the castle is located on a small peninsula, offering sweeping views and fantastic vistas over the brilliant turquoise waters of the Ionian Sea. Originally built by Ali Pasha Tepelena, Porto Palermo is a triangular shaped fort that served as a part fortress, part residence, and was wholly strategic. What makes it unique: Usually free from crowds, blessed with privacy, making for an intimate and serene visit. It sits on hidden beaches, with some of the most spectacular water found anywhere, opportune for exploration, kayaking or simply bear watching the waves crashing below the ancient battlements. Its remoteness and cinematic scenery have made it a popular spot for photographers and explorers. 6. Kanina Castle - An overlooked panorama Just above Vlora, where the Adriatic and Ionian Seas meet, is the lesser-known but beautiful Kanina Castle. The castle was originally built in the 3rd century BCE, originally expanded in Byzantine times and then reinforced by Skanderbeg himself. Why would you go to Kanina? You will enjoy amazing panoramic views over the Bay of Vlora, the Karaburun Peninsula, and on a clear day the coast of Italy. It is a quiet retreat - a good way to get to know Albania's history without the crowds. There is a somber beauty to the ruins and the surrounding countryside will entice hikers and tempt photographers. More Than Stone – A living experience with Albania Inbound These castles aren’t merely symbols of a lost time. They are dynamic gateways into the identity of Albania. Each conjures a different emotion and different rhythm, the pride of Kruja, the stillness of Berat, the mythology of Rozafa, the breezes of Porto Palermo.  At Albania Inbound we aspire to make these iconic places a personal journey. So whether you're seeking stories, vistas or a fuller understanding of this intriguing country, we welcome chance to guide your journey across more than just points of interest, but through the pulse of Albania. Ready to explore Albania's castles? Let Albania Inbound be your key.
posts.meta_title: Albania's castles - A journey of stone and time

posts.title: Weekend escapes from Tirana: Where to get away for 2 days?
posts.content:
The life we know in the capital, is dynamic and sometimes surprising, but every so often, we all need a little something different, and to clear our mental closet. Fortunately, Albania is small and diverse and it is easy to escape to the countryside without having to travel far. Whether it is to escape into the beauty of nature, a historical wander through interesting towns, or just a slice of rural authenticity, you can have the perfect weekend escape while being so close to Tirana.

Here are five memorable weekend escapes from Tirana, all will take you between a two and three hour drive from the capital.

  • Kruja: History with a view

Just 45 minutes north of Tirana lies the hilltop town of Kruja with the ideal combination of culture, history, and mountain air. Stroll through the cobblestone pavements of the old bazaar with many artisans who make handmade rugs, silver filigree, and traditional dress. Visit the Gjergj Kastrioti Skanderbeg Museum within the castle, who is Albania's national hero. The fortress walls have panoramic views of the plains and stretching out to the Adriatic Sea.

Where to stay: There are boutique guesthouses within the castle for an authentic Ottoman-era experience. Don't miss: A traditional lunch with mountain herbs and slow-cooked lamb at overlooking the valley from a terrace restaurant.  
  • Belsh: A town of a hundred lakes

Belsh is rarely on the radar of the traditional tourist, and is a hidden gem about 90 minutes from Tirana, in central Albania. Comprising of over 80 small lakes, Belsh has a peaceful disposition, providing a getaway for nature enthusiasts, cyclists, or those looking to unplug and rejuvenate. Kayaks skim quietly across still waters, hiking leads through olive groves, or simply enjoy the stillness of the Albanian countryside.

Where to stay: Eco-resorts, or agritourism farms with fresh local meals and basic but peaceful accommodation. Do not miss: sunset over Lake Seferan, arguably one of the most underrated views in the country.  
  • Divjaka-Karavasta National Park: Where wildlife and wilderness collide

Divjaka-Karavasta National Park's is bit over two hours drive from Tirana. It is a paradise for bird lovers or beachgoers. The park includes the largest lagoon in Albania and is home to endangered birds including the Dalmatian pelican. Wildlife watch in dense pine forests by bike ride, climb wooden observation towers or floating in a small boat in the lagoon.

Where to stay: Beach hotels or eco-lodges on the edge of a forest.

Don't Miss: A boat ride at sunrise in the lagoon. It very calm and misty while you sip your coffee, unforgettable!

 
  • Cape of Rodon: The Untouched Coast

Durres may be the default coast for Tirana visitors, and those willing to travel a bit more for off the beaten path will also want to travel north (1.5 hours) to the Cape of Rodon. The narrow peninsula is set into the Adriatic, and while it is remote, you will be amazed by the local nature nestled behind historic sites. Don't forget to visit the 15th-century Rodoni Castle, which is half sunken in the sea. Plan a secluded beach picnic at one of the cape's remote beaches complete with clear turquoise waters.

Where to stay: Adorable beach villas or guesthouses in the nearby villages such as Maminas.

Don't miss: The hike to the very point of the cape, where there land ends and the sea just goes on and on.

 
  • Pëllumbas and the Black Cave: An adventure so close to the city

Also only 45 minutes away, Pëllumbas is a small village; perfect for a spontaneous escape to nature. A short hike, along a pleasant track, will take you to the Black Cave (Shpella e Zezë), one of Albania's oldest Karst caves, inhabited since pre-history. The path to the cave offers amazing photo opportunities of the Erzen River valley, and the opportunity to enjoy some fresh mountain air.

Where to stay: Since Pëllumbas is so close, most people exit Tirana and return the same day, but there are family-run guesthouses so you can linger on into the night.   Don't miss: Take a swim in the Erzen river on a hot summers' day!

Escape with Albania Inbound

At Albania Inbound we believe the best way to discover the essence of Albania is through short but impactful escapes. Our crafted weekend packages include transportation, guided day tours, local experiences and comfortable accommodation, so you can take it easy and explore. As they say, it's a busy world, and we can accommodate anyone, whether you are coming from another corner of the world or you are a city-dweller wishing to take a break from the noise, we are always ready for your next adventure leave it to us and have your next mini-break planned! Our friendly team of travel specialists are here and would love to help you plan your perfect weekend getaway from Tirana!
posts.meta_title: Weekend escapes from Tirana: Where to get away for 2 days?

posts.title: 10 Reasons why Albania should be your next backpacking destination
posts.content:
If you are looking for an affordable, exciting backpacking trip, you need to travel to Albania. Tucked away in the middle of the Balkans, this diamond in the rough is an up-and-coming destination among adventurous travelers, and with good reason. Beautiful scenery, ornate culture, and friendly people make Albania perfect for what backpackers want, without the crowds and costs.

Here are 10 compelling reasons why Albania should be your next backpacking destination:

  • It’s crazy cheap Albania is one of the cheapest countries in Europe! Accommodation, transport, food, and activities cost a tiny fraction of what you'd pay in Western European countries. You could get a full meal for under €7, a hostel bed for €10-15 per night, and a beer for €1! Cheers to that!
  • The Albanian Riviera is breathtaking Think white sand beaches, turquoise water and sunsets that make the sunsets on the Greek islands look less stunning, and without crowds. From Dhërmi to Ksamil, if you like the beach you will find heaven on the Albanian Riviera. Swim, snorkel, kayak or do nothing or just sit by the sea and enjoy your favorite drink.
  • Genuine cultural experiences Albania definitely feels authentic, unlike many other over-touristed countries. Whether you're enjoying a cup of coffee in a local café, browsing through the bustle of village markets, or sipping raki with friendly Albanian locals, the hospitality will be present in all its true forms.
  • Amazing nature With the jagged summits of the Accursed Mountains in the north and the stunning lakes of Prespa and Shkodra, it is hard to find a destination that combines such incredible natural environments. Hill walk the famous Valbona to Theth, uncover hidden waterfalls or just wild camp under the stars!
  • Captivating history and distinct Architecture Albania has thousands of years of history to explore. You can visit UNESCO World Heritage Sites like Berat and Gjirokastër, the Greek Ruins of Butrint, explore Ottoman castles, and see Communist Bunkers in the Albanian countryside.
  • Friendly and hospitable people Albanians are known for their warmth and generosity, especially toward visitors. If someone invites you into their home to enjoy a coffee or a meal, don't be surprised, part of their culture is besa, the sacred code of hospitality.
  • Safe and easy to travel, especially with us Albania is a welcoming and safe destination, even for solo travelers. Public transportation is either affordable (if a bit adventurous!), and the distances are relatively short between the top destinations. But if you want to not have to worry about planning, and really enjoy yourself, then we can help. At Albania Inbound, we offer dependable transfers, small group tours and custom itineraries that let you explore Albania in comfort and confidence, without missing out on those hidden gems. Travel easy, travel local!
  • Underrated food scene Albanian food culture has a great combination of Mediterranean, Balkan and Turkish influences. Albanian food is delicious! Some local favorites include byrek, tavë kosi, grilled lamb and fresh seafood. Vegetarian and vegan options will also be abundant, especially with traditional home cooked meals.
  • Rich traditions and festivals Albania has a rich cultural calendar, from folk music to religious festivals. Timing your trip to coincide with Summer Day in Elbasan or the Gjirokastër Folklore Festival, or even just local wedding customs in the north, can help enhance your cultural experience.
  • Remains under the radar Albania is starting to get recognition, but it has yet to be inundated with large-scale tourism. That means UNESCO heritage sites, hiking trails in the mountains, and beautiful beaches are all things you can enjoy without crowds. This is a rare find when travelling in the contemporary world.

Travel with Albania Inbound

If you are looking for a way to enjoy your time in Albania with less of the stress of planning everything, consider small group tours with Albania Inbound. We provide a genuine experience in everything we do, including cultural walks through the cities, mountain hikes, and beach side adventures. Through our local Albanian guides, we provide a unique experience where you travel off the beaten path and engage with the places and people of Albania.

So what are you waiting for? Grab your backpack, break in those hiking boots and explore one of Europe’s best-kept secrets. Albania is waiting.

posts.title: Common mistakes made by tourists visiting Albania and how to avoid them?
posts.content:
Albania is becoming a popular destination in southeastern Europe with stunning beaches, wild mountains, and historic cities. But for many first-time visitors, their expectation doesn’t always match what they actually experience upon arrival. In this blog, we’ll focus on the most common mistakes tourists make when visiting Albania, and how to avoid them! It will help you have a great trip.

1. Assuming Albania is like any other Balkan country

One of the most common blunders is showing up with the idea that Albania is just like Greece, Montenegro, or Croatia. Albania has a unique culture, its own language, a complicated isolated history, an unusual set of traditions, and hospitality that cannot be replicated elsewhere. Do not categorize Albania in this way. Let the country shock you with its authenticity.

2. Underestimating the geography and infrastructure

A lot of tourists, especially those who rent cars, may not be prepared for the mountainous geography, the narrow streets in towns like Gjirokastër, or the lack of clear road signs in rural areas. GPS may even take you on paths which are not designed for a car but rather for goats!

Tip: If you are not an experienced driver on rough terrain, stick to the main roads, or choose to take guided tours.

3. Lack of understanding of the tipping culture Sometimes tourists feel uncomfortable when they are asked for "a coffee," which is a common way to request a tip. While tipping certainly isn't valued like it is in the U.S., in Albania you can simply leave a small amount for a waiter, taxi driver, or local guide.

Tip: Leaving about 10% is a totally acceptable way to show appreciation.

4. Only visiting Ksamil Albania, and not doing anything else

The other big error is only spending time on the southern beaches, and especially certainly Ksamil Albania, which has exploded across social media. But Albania has so much more: the Alps, the deep lakes of Theth and Valbona, Ottoman-era cities like Berat and Gjirokastër, and authentic villages away from the crowds.

Tip: Don't just think of Ksamil. Please plan for at least one historic town or mountain area to see the real balance of Albania.

5. Expecting everyone to speak English In cities and in touristy areas, many people will speak some English, particularly the younger generation. But if you travel into rural Albania or meet the older generation, you might hit a language barrier. More often than not this creates complications and wasted effort as you come to learn people often just don’t understand each other.

Tip- Learn a few words like: faleminderit (thank you), përshëndetje (hello), sa kushton? (how much?), or use translation apps. Albanians will appreciate your effort.

6. Believing it’s always sunny in Albania

Many guests, particularly those arriving in May or September, expect tropical type weather. Although Albania is Mediterranean and does not lack sunny days, rain and cool days can arrive too - particularly to the north, or when clients come to higher altitudes.

Tip: Always review the weather forecase before leaving home, and pack a light jacket or versatile clothes, just in case!

7. Forgetting it's still a developing country.

Sometimes tourists can feel let down by disorganisation, trash in some areas, or varying service levels, but it helps to keep in mind that Albania is still a developing nation, and it's a part of Albania's charm, unpolished, uncommercialized, and real.

Tip: Travel with no expectations. Give Albania a chance, and you'll find gems that you'll never encounter in a "perfected" Europe.

Traveling in Albania is an adventure, but it’s an adventure worth every step. With a little preparation and the right guidance, you can experience an extraordinary Albania, far from clichés and much closer to the truth. If you have questions, doubts, or simply want to feel guided, Albania Inbound is here for you, for every itinerary, every taste, and every pace.

Travel well. Travel with purpose. Travel with us.

posts.title: 5 Unusual experiences to enjoy in Albania this summer
posts.content:
When you think of a summer in Albania, you may envision the beautiful beaches in the south, enjoying some traditional delicious food, or visiting some nice cities with a wealth of history, however, in addition to this, Albania has a surprise and a little bit of adventure too. This summer, try to break away from the traditional, jot down five unusual experiences that will leave you astounded.

  • Kayaking on the Buna River, experience a different side of Shkodra

Kayaking is a beautiful way to experience Shkodra's nature in a relaxed and tranquil setting. With a slowly flowing river surrounded by lush vegetation, wetland birds, and quiet views that look like a painting, you'll find it is a gentle and enveloping way to connect with nature.

  • Camping in Llogara Forest, sleep under the stars

The Llogara pass is not just a pretty distance stop heading south, it is a world unto itself. Imagine waking up in the morning to fresh mountain and pine air, seeing more birds that you probably have all week in the city, surrounded by tents and campfires, and looking at the stars. Camping in Llogara is a wonderful way to immerse yourself in the wild and escape the trappings of city life.

  • Cycling along Lake Prespa. Innocence, nature, and calm

North Macedonia and Greece are just around thenostalgic corner, is the awe inspiring Lake Prespa, a hidden jewel tucked into the southeast of Albania. Lake Prespa reveals itself on a cycling tour amidst tranquil villages and pristine nature. This exploration along the coast means you can stop for a picnic on a cool spot by the lake, and taste the delicious fresh local fish as you meet extraordinary local people who are willing to tell you about their land and reveal their wonderful adventure.

  • Dinner at a Winery in Berat , where wine and heritage converge

Berat is not only the "city of a thousand windows", but it is also home to some of the most soul-stirring wine in Albania. You'll visit a family-run winery just outside the city, where you'll enjoy wine tastings directly from the vineyard, traditional dinner and enough local hospitality to make you feel like family – a delight for the senses!

  • Hike and explore Sazan Island, a lost military base now a paradise

Sazan Island was a military base, and now is home one of Albania’s more astonishing and wild places. Jump on a boat trip from Vlora to hidden beaches, abandoned bunkers and untouched nature. A small adventure that makes a big impression.

Are you ready to discover a different Albania? You will find that Albania has so much more to offer than typically found in travel brochures. These five experiences are only the tip of the iceberg, and if you want to organize the truly authentic, adventurous and memorable summer trips, Albania Inbound have guides, trips and enthusiasm for the hunt. So contact us today and let’s experience an Albania which so few have discovered, but many fall in love with.
posts.meta_title: 5 Unusual experiences to enjoy in Albania this summer

posts.title: The journey that changes you: Experiences you won't find in a travel guide to Albania
posts.content:
Some trips are about relaxation. Some are about adventure. But there are those rare journeys that change you. Albania is one of those places that, if you travel to it how it should be, has provide you with way more than just pretty images for Instagram. It provides you with memories that you will not find in any brochure, such as, unexpected encounters, emotional moments, and real human connections that you take with you after you finish your trip.

Here are a few experiences you will only have if you travel from the heart and not just a plan.

1. Sleeping in a Stone Tower in Theth

Waking up in a stone room, with the first rays of sunlight breaking over the Albanian Alps, is something words can hardly capture. The traditional “kullas” (stone towers) of Theth aren’t just beautiful buildings, they’re living witnesses of history, culture, and resilience.

At night, you’ll sit around the table with local families, sharing home-cooked meals made with love and ingredients straight from the land. You won’t just sleep in a bed , you’ll sleep inside a story.

 

2. Evening in Gjirokastër, accompanied by Polyphonic Singing

Guidebooks will tell you what to see in Gjirokastër, the castle, the old bazaar, the Ottoman houses. But none will explain to you how it feels. As the sun sets and the cobbled streets lose their tourists, the city starts to hum. Somewhere, in a bar that is supposed to be hidden, someone starts to sing polyphonic songs, raw, deep, ancestral. You are welcomed, even if uninvited. In that moment, you realize, Albania isn’t just a place, it’s a soul.

 

3. Making traditional bread in a Dropull village

If you want to understand a culture, start in the kitchen. And there’s nothing more real than making bread in a wood-fired oven with village women in southern Dropull. They might not speak your language, but when they knead the dough with rough hands and warm laughter, words aren’t needed.

These are not just moments to observe, they’re moments to live. And they’re what make a trip unforgettable.

 

4. Sunset among Flamingos in Divjakë

Although most individuals seek out sunsets on the beach, one of Albania's most enchanting sunsets is in the wetlands area of Divjakë, where soft, pink flamingos drift across still waters in groups. There is a strange silence, a silence full of life. You hear nothing but the colors of nature breathing. It is one of those moments you never plan for, it just happens, if you are there with your eyes open and have an open soul.

Why authentic experiences will always be more important than a resort?

Resorts will give you luxury but rarely will they give you emotion. These types of curated experiences, though often simple, offer something of value that cannot be bought: authenticity. In a world that is racing toward Instagrammed spaces, perhaps it is time to make the choice to travel toward something contributing to us more than from the outside-in.

Visit the real Albania, with Albania Inbound

At Albania Inbound we don't only offer you destinations, we offer you experience. Let us take you beyond the guidebooks and into the true nature, people, and spirit of Albania. Because the true journey is about discovering an experience which transforms you.
posts.meta_title: The journey that changes you: Experiences you won't find in a travel guide to Albania

posts.title: When Albania blooms: The perfect time to visit this hidden gem
posts.content:
There is a unique type of beauty in arriving somewhere at just the right time. In the case of Albania, that moment comes quietly. You will not hear about it on the news, nor will you see it trending on social media. But if you happen to stroll the streets of Tirana in late April, you will know when it comes. The city starts to breathe differently, the light becomes softer, café chairs appear again on sidewalks, and the mountains far on the horizon begin to melt into green once again. Albania comes to life in spring. After many months of winter, the air is soft and invigorating, not yet hot, but well on its way. May, in particular, feels like a treat. Temperatures have settled into the low to mid-20s°C, just warm enough to put your jacket away, but not hot enough that the sun becomes an inconvenience. The countryside comes alive, rivers filled with snowmelt, everything smells somehow cleaner. It is the best time to see Gjirokastër's stone-roofed houses and Berat's tranquil castle on the hill without breaking a sweat, for once. Yet, in the month of June, things are changing. The days get longer and, along with them, there comes a golden brightness to the coast of Albania. By the time July and August arrive, Albania is fully in summer. These are the months of heat, nightlife and movement. When average temperatures reach 30–35°C, everything revolves around water. The Albanian Riviera is a stretch of coast along the Ionian sea that is full of rocky coves and crystal-clear seas. From Dhërmi to Ksamil...the sun is bright, the music is cheery, the laughter is jovial, and the waves lap gentle against the beaches.     This is when Albania feels most Mediterranean; lengthy lunches under olive trees, fresh fish with local wine, and evenings that stretch into infinity. Ideal for those seeking the sun, but not suitable for anyone who tires of heat easily. Cities like Tirana and Shkodra can become quite hot in summer, so the coast is not just a destination, but a requirement. And just like that, it gets to the point where summer is fading. September rolls in, and with it comes balance. The sea is still warm, but the crowds are beginning to disperse. In the north, the hills start to change color, and the pace slows, just enough to notice. Many locals who love summer say this is the best time of the year to visit. The air is still filled with summer's sweetness, but the evenings are just slightly cooler, allowing you to stay outside a little longer. This is the perfect time to explore the ancient ruins of Butrint, swim in the peaceful waters of Saranda, or take a trip through the Llogara Pass where the mountains meet the sea.     Calm, calm, calm is what October continues. The coast quiets down, the forests on the way to Pogradec and Korçë turn gold, and the harvest season commences. Vineyards and orchards open to share the bounties of a long summer. It's an amazing time of food, photography and leisurely travel. Winter comes in slowly and quietly. In the lowlands, it is mild and slow. But in the mountains like Dardhë, or Voskopoja and Boge there are snow-covered villages, cozy inns, and a silence that makes you forget about time. It is not a heavy tourist season, which may be part of the charm. So, when should you visit Albania? The real question is: What type of Albania do you want to visit?
  • If you want flowers, quiet cities and the smell of fresh mountain air - visit in May.
  • If you want sun, sea, and everlasting energy - July and August are your months.
  • If you want warm light, warm waters and fewer people - pick September.
  • If you want silence, snow and reflection - consider January.
 All the seasons in Albania tell a different story. You just need to choose when you want to read the story. And when you are ready to turn the page, Albania Inbound will take you there. From tailor-made itineraries, to genuine local experiences, we help you find your season in Albania.
posts.meta_title: When Albania blooms: The perfect time to visit this hidden gem

posts.title: Amadeus Palace: The palace with no guests, Albania’s silent giant in gold and marble
posts.content:
f you are driving along the Tirana–Durrës highway, you will not be able to miss the Amadeus Palace. It rises from a flat plain of Albanian landscape like one more misplaced royal fantasy, more like still from the back of a middle eastern fairytale than any reality. Gold plated domes, endless rows of arches, polished white marble, balconies with columns, it is breath-taking, surreal, and inexplicably strange. But, and here is the fun part, it is completely empty.

The first stone of Amadeus Palace was laid in 2011, when Albania was riding high on ambitious new architectural plans to create mega-monuments and touristic attractions by inviting foreign investment into its developing tourism economy. This was meant to be among the country’s proudest attractions - a luxurious five-star hotel that was meant to drive the Albanian tourism market. And in appearance, it did just that. It is a monument of bling, adorned with a valiant posture gleaming in the sun. But beyond its gates, silence reigns.

The Architecture: Between fantasy and identity

Amadeus Palace is an architectural puzzle. It does not fit into one particular style, it fuses elements of neo-Baroque, oriental influences with neoclassical elements, creating a theatrical semblance of imperial exaggeration.
